g.data-narration: Welcome to our lecture on Decentralized Autonomous Organizations. We'll explore how blockchain is revolutionizing organizational governance.
A DAO is a blockchain-based organization governed by smart contracts, where members collectively own and manage the entity through democratic voting.
DAOs operate on four core principles: transparency through public blockchains, immutability of rules, token-based governance, and collective decision-making.
Unlike traditional hierarchical organizations with CEOs and boards, DAOs are flat structures where every member has equal voting power and direct participation.
DAOs operate through a layered architecture: blockchain provides the foundation, smart contracts encode rules, tokens enable governance, and proposals drive decisions.
Smart contracts automate DAO operations by executing code when conditions are met, handling token transfers, vote counting, and fund management autonomously.
g.text: Smart Contracts: The Brain of a DAO

g.data-narration: Token-based voting is the most common DAO governance, where each token represents one vote, allowing proportional influence based on token holdings.
g.text: Alice: 100 tokens | Bob: 50 tokens | Carol: 25 tokens

g.data-narration: Advanced governance includes quadratic voting (reducing wealthy influence) and conviction voting (time-weighted decisions), addressing limitations of simple token voting.
g.text: Cost = Votes² — Reduces wealthy minority dominance
Time-staked votes — Longer commitment = More influence

g.data-narration: A DAO follows a lifecycle: token distribution establishes initial governance, proposals initiate decisions, voting determines outcomes, and execution enacts results.
g.text: DAO Lifecycle: From Creation to Execution

g.data-narration: DAOs offer significant advantages: global participation removes geographic barriers, transparency builds trust, reduced costs eliminate intermediaries, and 24/7 operations enable rapid decisions.
DAOs face challenges including code vulnerabilities exploited by hackers, governance apathy where members don't vote, regulatory uncertainty, and slow decision-making on complex issues.
g.text: 💡 Mitigations: Audits, Incentivized Voting, Legal Wrappers, Delegation

g.data-narration: DAOs power diverse applications: DeFi protocols for protocol governance, investment clubs for pooled capital, grant programs for community funding, and social clubs for member coordination.
